attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a 10-day history of headaches. The headache is dull and centred over the vertex. It is variable and sometimes pounding and tends to be made worse by movement. He occasionally feels nauseated when the headache is present and is unable to continue with what he is doing, though has not taken any medication for his symptoms. About two weeks ago, he tripped and fell backwards in the garden, hitting his head on some brick paving. He did not lose consciousness and remembers the event clearly. There was no ante- or retrograde amnesia. Initially, he did not have any symptoms. He has occasional stress headaches and takes paracetamol for the headaches once per month with good relief. He smokes 30 cigarettes per day for the last 38 years. He does not take any regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. He is oriented and no focal neurological deficits are found on cranial nerve and neurological examination. There is some old bruising on the back of his head but no other signs of trauma.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with new-onset headaches following head trauma two weeks ago. While post-traumatic headaches are common and can persist for weeks to months, the progressive nature of his symptoms with associated nausea warrants urgent investigation to exclude intracranial complications such as subdural or epidural haematoma.

Non-contrast CT head is the most appropriate initial investigation. It is highly sensitive for detecting acute and subacute intracranial haemorrhage, which is the primary concern given the history of head trauma. Non-contrast CT is readily available, quick to perform, and provides excellent visualisation of blood products, making it the gold standard for evaluating post-traumatic intracranial bleeding.

CT angiography of head would be indicated if there was suspicion of vascular injury such as carotid or vertebral artery dissection, or if looking for aneurysms. However, there are no clinical features suggesting vascular pathology, and the recent head trauma makes haemorrhage the primary concern.

CT venography of head is used to evaluate cerebral venous thrombosis, which typically presents with headache but is not associated with trauma. The clinical presentation and history do not suggest venous sinus thrombosis.

MRI brain provides excellent soft tissue detail and is more sensitive than CT for certain pathologies. However, it is not the first-line investigation for suspected intracranial haemorrhage, is less readily available, takes longer to perform, and may miss acute blood products that CT would easily detect.

MR angiogram is used to evaluate cerebral vasculature and would be indicated if there was suspicion of vascular abnormalities such as aneurysms or arteriovenous malformations. Like CT angiography, it is not the appropriate first-line investigation in the context of recent head trauma where intracranial haemorrhage is the primary concern.

If the non-contrast CT head reveals intracranial pathology, urgent neurosurgical consultation would be warranted. If normal, management would depend on clinical judgement, potentially including symptomatic treatment for post-traumatic headache or further investigation if symptoms persist or worsen.
learningPoints: An acute headache after recent head-strike should be a cause of concern and prompt further investigation.
Non-contrast CT head is the first-line imaging for suspected intracranial haemorrhage following head trauma.
Post-traumatic headaches can persist for weeks to months after head injury, but new or worsening symptoms warrant urgent investigation.
references: Rau JC, Dumkrieger GM, Chong CD, Schwedt TJ. Imaging Post-Traumatic Headache. Curr Pain Headache Rep. 2018 Jul 30;22(10):64. doi: 10.1007/s11916-018-0719-z. PMID: 30062548.
Stupar M, Kim PS. Delayed-onset post-traumatic headache after a motor vehicle collision: a case report. J Can Chiropr Assoc. 2007 Jun;51(2):83-90. PMID: 17657301; PMCID: PMC1924661.
Epidural Hematoma in Emergency Medicine at Medscape. Author: Daniel D Price. Updated: Nov 3, 2010

Khairat A, Waseem M. Epidural Hematoma. [Updated 2022 May 9]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK518982/
